cheatSheet:
## Spring Boot Cheat Sheet

### Core Annotations
| Annotation | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| @SpringBootApplication | Main class (Config + AutoConfig + Scan) |
| @Component | Generic bean |
| @Service | Business logic bean |
| @Repository | Data access bean |
| @RestController | REST endpoints |
| @Configuration | Java-based config class |
| @Bean | Method-level bean definition |

### Dependency Injection
| Annotation | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| @Autowired | Inject dependency (prefer constructor) |
| @Qualifier | Disambiguate multiple beans |
| @Value | Inject property value |
| @Primary | Default bean when multiple candidates |
| @Scope | singleton, prototype, request, session |

### REST API
| Annotation | HTTP Method |
|------------|-------------|
| @GetMapping | GET |
| @PostMapping | POST |
| @PutMapping | PUT |
| @DeleteMapping | DELETE |
| @RequestBody | Deserialize JSON body |
| @PathVariable | Extract from URL path |
| @RequestParam | Extract query parameter |

### JPA / Data
| Annotation | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| @Entity | Maps class to DB table |
| @Id + @GeneratedValue | Primary key |
| @OneToMany, @ManyToOne | Relationships |
| @Transactional | Transaction boundary |
| JpaRepository | CRUD + paging + sorting |

### Security
- JWT: stateless auth, Bearer token
- SecurityFilterChain: configure routes, CORS, CSRF
- @PreAuthorize: method-level security
- BCryptPasswordEncoder: password hashing

### Testing
| Annotation | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| @SpringBootTest | Full integration test |
| @WebMvcTest | Controller layer only |
| @DataJpaTest | Repository layer only |
| @MockBean | Replace bean with mock |

### Actuator
- /actuator/health, /actuator/metrics, /actuator/info, /actuator/env

### Quick Reference — Java

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Spring Boot quick reference
        System.out.println("=== Spring Boot ===");
        
        // Core data structure
        Map<String, Object> config = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        config.put("topic", "Spring Boot");
        config.put("complexity", "O(n)");
        
        for (var e : config.entrySet())
            System.out.println(e.getKey() + ": " + e.getValue());
    }
}
```

### Quick Reference — Python

```python
# Spring Boot quick reference
config = {
    "topic": "Spring Boot",
    "complexity": "O(n)",
}

for key, val in config.items():
    print(f"{key}: {val}")
```

levels.title: Shishya
levels.description: Can create a Spring Boot app, understand starters and auto-configuration basics

plan.overview: Master Spring Boot for backend interviews in 10 focused sessions covering auto-configuration, REST, JPA, security, microservices, testing, caching, error handling, and production deployment.
plan.skippedTopics: Spring Batch, Spring Integration, Spring WebFlux (reactive), Spring AMQP deep dive — these are niche topics rarely tested in general backend interviews.

sessions.title: Spring Boot Basics
sessions.objective: Understand auto-configuration, starters, and application properties

activities.description: Create a Hello World app with spring initializr
Override default port and add a custom property
Inspect auto-configured beans with --debug flag

reviewQuestions: What does @SpringBootApplication combine?
How does auto-configuration decide which beans to create?
What is the difference between application.properties and application.yml?
reviewAnswers: @SpringBootApplication combines @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan into one annotation.
Auto-configuration uses @Conditional annotations to create beans only when certain classes are on the classpath or properties are set.
Both configure the same properties; YAML supports nested structure and lists more cleanly, but both are equivalent in capability.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of Spring Boot Basics
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Spring Boot Basics
Apply Spring Boot Basics concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: Dependency Injection & IoC
sessions.objective: Master Spring's IoC container, bean scopes, and DI patterns

activities.description: Refactor field injection to constructor injection
Create a prototype-scoped bean and verify new instances
Use @Qualifier to select between two implementations

reviewQuestions: Why is constructor injection preferred over field injection?
What is the default bean scope in Spring?
What is the difference between @Component and @Bean?
reviewAnswers: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, supports immutability (final fields), and makes unit testing easier without a Spring context.
Singleton — one shared instance per Spring container for the lifetime of the application.
@Component is a class-level annotation for auto-detection via component scan. @Bean is a method-level annotation inside @Configuration classes for explicit bean creation.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of Dependency Injection & IoC
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Dependency Injection & IoC
Apply Dependency Injection & IoC concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: REST APIs
sessions.objective: Build robust REST APIs with validation, proper status codes, and error responses

activities.description: Build a CRUD REST API for a resource
Add @Valid to a POST endpoint and test with invalid payload
Return 201 Created with Location header on resource creation

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between @Controller and @RestController?
How do you return a 404 status from a REST controller?
How does Bean Validation integrate with Spring MVC?
reviewAnswers: @RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody, so every method automatically serializes the return value to JSON without needing @ResponseBody on each method.
Return ResponseEntity.notFound().build() or throw a ResponseStatusException(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND).
Annotate the DTO with validation constraints (@NotNull, etc.) and add @Valid on the @RequestBody parameter. Spring triggers validation and throws MethodArgumentNotValidException on failure.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of REST APIs
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to REST APIs
Apply REST APIs concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: Spring Data JPA
sessions.objective: Persist entities using JPA repositories, understand relationships and query methods

activities.description: Create an @Entity with a @OneToMany relationship
Write a derived query and a @Query JPQL query
Paginate results using Pageable

reviewQuestions: What is the N+1 query problem and how do you solve it?
What does @Transactional do and where should it be placed?
What is the difference between JPQL and native SQL queries in Spring Data?
reviewAnswers: N+1 occurs when loading a parent entity triggers N additional queries for child entities. Solve with JOIN FETCH in JPQL or @EntityGraph to load associations in one query.
@Transactional wraps the method in a database transaction, rolling back on RuntimeException. It should be placed on service layer methods, not repositories or controllers.
JPQL operates on entity objects and field names (portable across databases). Native queries use raw SQL syntax and are database-specific but allow DB-specific features.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of Spring Data JPA
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Spring Data JPA
Apply Spring Data JPA concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: Spring Security
sessions.objective: Implement authentication and authorization using JWT and OAuth2

activities.description: Implement JWT authentication filter
Secure endpoints with role-based access
Hash a password with BCrypt and verify

reviewQuestions: How does JWT authentication work in Spring Security?
What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
Why must passwords be hashed and not encrypted?
reviewAnswers: A custom OncePerRequestFilter extracts and validates the JWT from the Authorization header, then sets a UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken in the SecurityContextHolder, granting access.
Authentication verifies identity (who are you?). Authorization checks permissions (what are you allowed to do?). Spring Security handles both via filters and access control.
Hashing is one-way — even if the database is compromised, plaintext passwords cannot be recovered. Encryption is reversible, so a stolen key exposes all passwords.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of Spring Security
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Spring Security
Apply Spring Security concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: Microservices
sessions.objective: Build microservices with service discovery, config server, and API gateway

activities.description: Set up Eureka Server and register two services
Create a Feign Client to call another microservice
Add a circuit breaker with Resilience4j fallback

reviewQuestions: What problem does a service registry solve?
What is a circuit breaker and when does it open?
What is the difference between a Feign Client and a RestTemplate?
reviewAnswers: A service registry (Eureka) allows services to find each other by name without hardcoded URLs, enabling dynamic scaling and failover.
A circuit breaker monitors failure rates. When failures exceed a threshold, it opens and routes requests to a fallback, preventing cascading failures across services.

Feign is declarative — you define an interface and Spring generates the HTTP client. RestTemplate is imperative — you write the HTTP call manually. Feign is cleaner and integrates with load balancing.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of Microservices
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Microservices
Apply Microservices concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: Testing
sessions.objective: Write unit, slice, and integration tests using JUnit 5, Mockito, and MockMvc

activities.description: Write a @WebMvcTest for a controller
Mock a service using @MockBean
Write a @DataJpaTest for a repository query

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between @SpringBootTest and @WebMvcTest?
What is @MockBean and how does it differ from Mockito.mock()?
When would you use Testcontainers over H2?
reviewAnswers: @SpringBootTest loads the full application context for integration tests. @WebMvcTest loads only the web layer (controllers, filters) and is faster for testing REST endpoints in isolation.
@MockBean creates a Mockito mock and registers it in the Spring context, replacing the real bean. Mockito.mock() creates a mock outside the Spring context for plain unit tests.
Use Testcontainers when you need to test database-specific behavior (PostgreSQL features, stored procedures, migrations) that H2 cannot replicate.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of Testing
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Testing
Apply Testing concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: Caching & Performance
sessions.objective: Add caching with @Cacheable, configure Redis, and tune HikariCP connection pool

activities.description: Add @Cacheable to a service method and verify caching
Evict cache on update with @CacheEvict
Configure Redis as cache manager in application.properties

reviewQuestions: What does @Cacheable do and what is its default key?
When should you use Redis over in-memory caching?
What are the risks of setting HikariCP pool size too high?
reviewAnswers: @Cacheable intercepts the method, checks the cache for the key (default: method parameters), returns cached value if found, or executes the method and stores the result.
Use Redis when you have multiple application instances (in-memory cache is not shared), or when you need cache persistence across restarts.
Too many connections overwhelm the database (which has its own connection limits), cause lock contention, and consume memory. More connections doesn't always mean better performance.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of Caching & Performance
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Caching & Performance
Apply Caching & Performance concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: Error Handling, Actuator & Logging
sessions.objective: Handle exceptions globally, monitor with Actuator, and configure structured logging

activities.description: Create a @ControllerAdvice that returns RFC 7807 problem details
Expose /actuator/health and /actuator/metrics via properties
Add MDC request ID to all log lines via a filter

reviewQuestions: How does @ControllerAdvice differ from try-catch in controllers?
What is Micrometer and how does it relate to Actuator?
Why use SLF4J instead of directly using Logback?
reviewAnswers: @ControllerAdvice is a global exception handler — one class handles exceptions for all controllers, ensuring consistent error format without duplicating try-catch blocks.
Micrometer is a vendor-neutral metrics facade (like SLF4J for logging). Actuator uses Micrometer to collect metrics and expose them to monitoring systems like Prometheus.
SLF4J is an abstraction — your code stays independent of the underlying logging framework. You can swap Logback for Log4j2 without changing application code.
objectives: Understand the core concepts of Error Handling, Actuator & Logging
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Error Handling, Actuator & Logging
Apply Error Handling, Actuator & Logging concepts to real-world system design scenarios

sessions.title: Production: Docker, Profiles & Health Checks
sessions.objective: Package and deploy Spring Boot apps with Docker, use profiles for environments, and configure health checks

activities.description: Create a multi-stage Dockerfile with layered JAR
Define dev and prod profiles with different DB configs
Configure K8s liveness and readiness probes using Actuator

reviewQuestions: What are Spring Profiles and why are they useful?
What is the benefit of a layered JAR in Docker?
What is the difference between liveness and readiness probes in K8s?
reviewAnswers: Profiles allow different configurations for different environments (dev, test, prod) — separate database URLs, logging levels, feature flags — activated at runtime without code changes.
Layered JARs separate dependencies (rarely changing) from application code (frequently changing) into different Docker layers, so rebuilds only push the changed layer, saving time and bandwidth.

Liveness probe checks if the app is alive (restart if dead). Readiness probe checks if the app is ready to receive traffic (remove from load balancer if not ready, e.g., during startup or DB reconnection).

codeExamples.java:
# application-prod.properties
spring.datasource.url=${DATABASE_URL}
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=20
logging.level.root=WARN
management.endpoint.health.show-details=never

# Dockerfile
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY target/*.jar app.jar
RUN java -Djarmode=layertools -jar app.jar extract

FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dependencies/ ./
COPY --from=builder /app/application/ ./
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]

objectives: Understand the core concepts of Production: Docker, Profiles & Health Checks
Implement a working solution in Java and Python
Analyze time and space complexity
Identify common interview questions related to Production: Docker, Profiles & Health Checks
Apply Production: Docker, Profiles & Health Checks concepts to real-world system design scenarios

quizBank.question: Which annotation combination does @SpringBootApplication replace?
quizBank.explanation: @SpringBootApplication is a meta-annotation that combines @Configuration (marks the class as a bean source), @ComponentScan (enables component scanning), and @EnableAutoConfiguration (triggers Spring Boot's auto-configuration based on classpath contents).

quizBank.question: Why is constructor injection preferred over field injection in Spring?
options: It supports immutability and makes dependencies explicit and testable
It works without the Spring container
quizBank.correctAnswer: It supports immutability and makes dependencies explicit and testable
quizBank.explanation: Constructor injection allows dependencies to be declared as final (immutable), makes required dependencies explicit in the constructor signature, and enables unit testing without a Spring context — you simply call the constructor with mocks. Field injection hides dependencies and requires reflection or a Spring context to inject.

quizBank.question: What HTTP status code should a successful POST that creates a resource return?
quizBank.explanation: 201 Created is the correct status for a successful resource creation. The response should also include a Location header pointing to the new resource's URL. 200 OK is for successful reads or updates that return a body. 204 No Content is for successful operations with no response body (e.g., DELETE).

quizBank.question: What is the N+1 query problem in Spring Data JPA?
options: Running N queries in a @Transactional method causes rollback
Fetching a list of N entities triggers N additional queries to load a lazy association
Using N @Autowired fields causes injection failure
Pagination with N pages requires N+1 database connections
quizBank.correctAnswer: Fetching a list of N entities triggers N additional queries to load a lazy association
quizBank.explanation: When you fetch a list of N parent entities and then access a lazily-loaded association on each one, JPA executes 1 query for parents + N queries for children = N+1 total. Fix with JOIN FETCH in JPQL (loads everything in one query) or @EntityGraph to specify associations to eagerly load.

quizBank.question: What does @Transactional do when placed on a service method?
options: It caches the method result for subsequent calls
It wraps the method in a database transaction, committing on success and rolling back on RuntimeException
It synchronizes the method to prevent concurrent access
It logs all database queries made by the method
quizBank.correctAnswer: It wraps the method in a database transaction, committing on success and rolling back on RuntimeException
quizBank.explanation: Spring creates a proxy around @Transactional methods. On entry, it begins a transaction; on normal return, it commits; on unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException and Error), it rolls back. Checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback by default — configure with rollbackFor if needed.

quizBank.question: In Spring Security, where should the JWT be validated in the request lifecycle?
options: In a OncePerRequestFilter before the security filter chain processes authorization
In an @Aspect around all controller methods
quizBank.correctAnswer: In a OncePerRequestFilter before the security filter chain processes authorization
quizBank.explanation: A OncePerRequestFilter runs once per request. The JWT filter extracts the token from the Authorization header, validates signature and expiry, loads user details, and sets a UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken in the SecurityContextHolder. This happens before authorization checks, allowing Spring Security to make access decisions based on the authenticated principal.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of a circuit breaker in a microservices architecture?
options: To prevent cascading failures by stopping calls to a failing service and returning a fallback
To load balance requests evenly across service instances
To centralize logging from all microservices
quizBank.correctAnswer: To prevent cascading failures by stopping calls to a failing service and returning a fallback
quizBank.explanation: A circuit breaker monitors failure rates to a downstream service. When failures exceed a threshold, it opens — subsequent calls skip the failing service and immediately execute a fallback method. This prevents thread pool exhaustion and cascading failures across the whole system. Resilience4j is the standard library for this in Spring Boot.

quizBank.question: Which test annotation loads ONLY the web layer without a full application context?
quizBank.explanation: @WebMvcTest loads only the web layer: controllers, filters, @ControllerAdvice, and MockMvc. It does NOT load @Service or @Repository beans — you must provide mocks via @MockBean. This makes it fast and focused on testing controller logic. @SpringBootTest loads the full context, and @DataJpaTest loads only the JPA layer.

quizBank.question: What does @Cacheable(value = "users", key = "#id") do?
options: It stores every method call result in the 'users' cache with the id as key, returning cached results on repeated calls
It caches only the first call and ignores subsequent calls
It creates a Redis cache named 'users' automatically
It validates that the id parameter exists in the users table
quizBank.correctAnswer: It stores every method call result in the 'users' cache with the id as key, returning cached results on repeated calls
quizBank.explanation: On the first call, Spring executes the method and stores the result in the cache named 'users' with the key equal to the id parameter. On subsequent calls with the same id, Spring returns the cached value without executing the method. The cache can be in-memory (default) or external like Redis when configured.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between liveness and readiness probes in Kubernetes?
options: Liveness checks CPU usage; readiness checks memory usage
Liveness determines if the container should be restarted; readiness determines if it should receive traffic
Liveness is for HTTP apps; readiness is for TCP apps
Liveness probes run at startup; readiness probes run continuously
quizBank.correctAnswer: Liveness determines if the container should be restarted; readiness determines if it should receive traffic
quizBank.explanation: A failed liveness probe causes K8s to kill and restart the container (the app is stuck/deadlocked). A failed readiness probe removes the pod from the service's endpoint list so no traffic is routed to it, but the container is not restarted. Spring Boot Actuator exposes /actuator/health/liveness and /actuator/health/readiness as separate endpoints for these probes.

quizBank.question: What is the primary advantage of Spring Boot?
options: D) All of the above, depending on context
quizBank.explanation: Spring Boot offers different advantages depending on the use case. In interviews, always discuss trade-offs rather than absolving advantages.

quizBank.question: When should you NOT use Spring Boot?
options: B) When the team is small
C) When the overhead exceeds the benefit
quizBank.explanation: Every technology has trade-offs. Spring Boot adds complexity that may not be justified for simple use cases. Always evaluate if the overhead is worth the benefit.

quizBank.question: What is the time complexity of the core operation in Spring Boot?
options: D) It depends on the implementation
quizBank.explanation: The time complexity depends on the specific implementation and operation. In interviews, always clarify which operation before stating complexity.

quizBank.question: How does Spring Boot handle failures?
options: D) Strategy depends on the system requirements
quizBank.explanation: Failure handling in Spring Boot depends on requirements: retry for transient failures, circuit breaker for cascading failures, graceful degradation for user-facing systems.

quizBank.question: Which company popularized the use of Spring Boot?
quizBank.explanation: Spring Boot evolved through contributions from multiple companies. In interviews, mentioning real-world examples from different companies shows breadth of knowledge.
